title: Signed Offline Bundle Supply-Chain SLO (Redwood Private)
preview:
## Purpose
This document defines reliability targets (SLOs), monitoring, and escalation paths for the **offline bundle signing and publication pipeline** used by Redwood Private (VPC + on‑prem / air‑gapped). The objective is to keep customer deployments unblocked by signing-related issues while maintaining strong fa...
text:
## Purpose
This document defines reliability targets (SLOs), monitoring, and escalation paths for the **offline bundle signing and publication pipeline** used by Redwood Private (VPC + on‑prem / air‑gapped). The objective is to keep customer deployments unblocked by signing-related issues while maintaining strong fail-closed security properties.

This page is owned by **Eng SRE** with shared accountability across **Release Engineering**, **Build/CI**, and **Security Engineering**.

## Scope
In scope:
- Deterministic build + manifest generation for Redwood Private offline bundles.
- Cryptographic signing of the bundle manifest (and/or signature envelope) using Redwood-controlled keys.
- Publishing of signed bundle artifacts to approved distribution endpoints (internal artifact store + customer delivery mechanism).
- Verification gating required for cutting a Private release.
- Telemetr...

text:
## Purpose
This runbook covers on-call response for overload events related to **Hot-route capacity protection** on Redwood Inference’s Hosted API and Dedicated tiers.

It helps you:
- Diagnose whether we are in **quota/rate limit** vs **overload admission control / shedding**.
- Interpret new signals (queue depth, scheduler lag, shed rates, 429 reason codes).
- Take safe mitigation actions that protect enterprise SLOs while minimizing fleet-wide degradation.

## Scope
Applies to the following high-traffic routes and route groups:
- `/v1/chat/completions`
- `/v1/embeddings`
- `/v1/rerank`
- Any configured **protected route group** (see Dashboard + Policy config).

This runbook assumes enforcement occurs at:
- **API Gateway** (tier-aware rate limiting/quota evaluation, consistent 429 semantics)
- **Routing service** (priority routing + admission control under pressure)
- **Serving runt...

text:
## Purpose
This page defines how **Q1 2026 performance baseline metrics** (bench harness / perf-canary) map to **production latency SLO dashboards and alerting**.

The goal is to prevent two common failure modes:
1) Treating benchmark deltas as production incidents (false pages).
2) Ignoring benchmark deltas that predict real user-facing SLO risk.

This is an alignment doc (definitions + mapping + operational policy). It does **not** replace:
- The baseline methodology (see Q1 2026 perf baseline plan).
- The company-wide perf regression policy/thresholds.

## Definitions (what we measure)
### Baseline / perf-suite metrics (engineering benchmark semantics)
Perf-suite runs are controlled, reproducible, and carry a run manifest (ADR-0172). Key metrics:
- **TTFT (Time To First Token)**: server-side time from request accepted by Redwood API gateway (benchmark client uses direct internal en...

title: Postmortem: Controlled failover test regression (us-east → eu-west) – transient 5xx + tail latency
preview:
## Summary
On **2026-01-15**, during a **planned controlled regional failover test** (Hosted API and a limited Dedicated slice) from **us-east** to **eu-west**, we introduced a **customer-visible regression**. A sequence of noisy regional-impairment signals and insufficient rollback/anti-flap guardrails caused repea...
text:
## Summary
On **2026-01-15**, during a **planned controlled regional failover test** (Hosted API and a limited Dedicated slice) from **us-east** to **eu-west**, we introduced a **customer-visible regression**. A sequence of noisy regional-impairment signals and insufficient rollback/anti-flap guardrails caused repeated routing policy changes (“flip/rollback”) and a transient overload condition in the target region.

**Customer impact:**
- **Hosted API:** elevated 5xx/timeouts and tail latency for a subset of routes while traffic re-converged.
- **Dedicated:** minimal direct impact due to scoped tenants, but some control-plane operations (drain workflow status) were delayed.
- **Streaming:** some clients experienced disconnects on redirect and did not recover cleanly without application-level retries.

**Why this postmortem exists:** This was an intentional test. The regression was not...

title: Proactive anomaly quarantine and capacity hedging runbook
preview:
Summary:

This runbook describes the proactive anomaly quarantine and capacity hedging process used by Redwood Inference SRE to detect early signals of service instability, isolate affected traffic (quarantine), and allocate short-term capacity hedges to preserve SLOs while a remediation is performed. It ties direct...
text:
Summary:

This runbook describes the proactive anomaly quarantine and capacity hedging process used by Redwood Inference SRE to detect early signals of service instability, isolate affected traffic (quarantine), and allocate short-term capacity hedges to preserve SLOs while a remediation is performed. It ties directly to SLO thresholds and error budget policies and is intended for oncall, incident commanders, and automation owners.

Scope:
- Applies to customer-facing inference endpoints (hosted API) and inter-service routing between control-plane components and serving runtime.
- Not a replacement for full incident response; this is a containment and mitigation layer used before escalation to a SEV.

Why this exists:
- Transient anomalies (kernel/backpressure, noisy tenants, model-level regressions) can erode SLOs quickly. Rapid isolation + capacity hedging preserves customer experie...

title: Tabletop: SLO-to-Capacity Runway Playbook

text:
Summary
-------
This playbook describes how to design, run, and learn from SLO-aligned tabletop simulations that trace an outage hypothesis from customer-visible impact through telemetry, error-budget consumption, and capacity runway decisions. The focus is practical: runbooks, PromQL examples, decision thresholds, and post-exercise actions that connect incident review findings to capacity planning and reliability initiatives.

Scope
-----
- Services: inference-api, routing-service, model-cache, realtime-ingest, dedicated-proxy
- Scenarios: latency tail regression, KV cache invalidation, cold-start surge, multi-tenant eviction cascade, model-quality regression causing retries
- Participants: SRE on-call, service owner, product PM, platform capacity engineer, customer success (for customer-impact simulations)

Goals
-----
1. Validate that SLO alerting maps to actionable capacity thresh...

title: Hosted API SLOs: Enterprise Route Tiers (Hot-route capacity protection)
preview:
## Purpose
This document defines **per-route SLOs and error budget policy** for Redwood Inference’s **Hosted API** as it relates to **hot-route capacity protection** (tier-aware quotas, priority routing, and controlled shedding).

The intent is to:
- Explicitly define **enterprise vs best-effort** expectations for t...
text:
## Purpose
This document defines **per-route SLOs and error budget policy** for Redwood Inference’s **Hosted API** as it relates to **hot-route capacity protection** (tier-aware quotas, priority routing, and controlled shedding).

The intent is to:
- Explicitly define **enterprise vs best-effort** expectations for the highest-traffic routes.
- Make overload behavior **measurable and supportable** (clear SLIs + dashboards + alerts).
- Align alerting to overload protection so we page on **enterprise degradation**, not expected best-effort shedding.

## Scope
**In scope**
- Hosted API routes: `/v1/chat/completions`, `/v1/embeddings`, `/v1/rerank`, `/v1/completions`.
- Tiered behavior: **Enterprise (Protected)** vs **Best-effort (Standard/Self-serve)**.
- SLOs for **availability**, **latency**, and **overload responses (429)**.
- Alert thresholds and error budget burn policy.

**Out of sc...

title: Incident taxonomy + ownership mapping — Program kickoff (Reliability Program)
preview:
## Summary
This page kicks off the Reliability Program initiative “Incident taxonomy + ownership mapping”. The goal is to reduce incident frequency and improve MTTR by standardizing (1) incident classification, (2) owner accountability, and (3) remediation expectations (SLA timers) across Hosted, Dedicated, and Priv...
text:
## Summary
This page kicks off the Reliability Program initiative “Incident taxonomy + ownership mapping”. The goal is to reduce incident frequency and improve MTTR by standardizing (1) incident classification, (2) owner accountability, and (3) remediation expectations (SLA timers) across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private tiers.

We are targeting a taxonomy that is:
- Easy to apply live during an incident (decision-tree friendly)
- Low-cardinality and stable (safe for metric labels and dashboards)
- Aligned with real operational control boundaries (single primary owner per type)
- Useful for reporting, prioritization, and error budget breakdowns

## Why we’re doing this
Current incident labeling is inconsistent (freeform tags, service-only naming, inconsistent ownership), which causes:
- Ambiguity during response (who owns mitigation vs who is consulted)
- Postmortems that are hard to ro...

title: Incident taxonomy v1: definitions, subtypes, and on-call decision tree
preview:
## Purpose
This page defines the **Incident Taxonomy v1** used across incident-bot, dashboards, postmortems, and reporting. The taxonomy is designed to be:
- **Easy to apply during a live incident** (symptom-first, not root-cause-first)
- **Low-cardinality and stable** (safe as a metric label allowlist)
- **Consiste...
text:
## Purpose
This page defines the **Incident Taxonomy v1** used across incident-bot, dashboards, postmortems, and reporting. The taxonomy is designed to be:
- **Easy to apply during a live incident** (symptom-first, not root-cause-first)
- **Low-cardinality and stable** (safe as a metric label allowlist)
- **Consistent for reporting** (incident volume/impact by type and owner)

**How to use:** During the incident, select the **top-level incident_type** and **subtype** that best matches the *primary customer-visible symptom* at the time of peak impact.

If multiple things are true (e.g., capacity event causes routing fallback), choose the **primary type** based on the decision rules below and record the rest under **contributing systems** in the incident timeline/postmortem.

---

## Taxonomy model (what gets recorded)
- **incident_type**: One of the top-level categories below (allowlis...

title: Hosted circuit breakers + load shedding (Reliability Program) — Overview
preview:
## Summary
Redwood Inference’s Hosted API experiences bursty, highly variable workloads (prompt-length variance, streaming, and customer traffic spikes). When capacity or dependencies saturate, overload can cascade into tail-latency blowups, elevated 5xx rates, and noisy-neighbor effects.

This program adds explicit...
text:
## Summary
Redwood Inference’s Hosted API experiences bursty, highly variable workloads (prompt-length variance, streaming, and customer traffic spikes). When capacity or dependencies saturate, overload can cascade into tail-latency blowups, elevated 5xx rates, and noisy-neighbor effects.

This program adds explicit overload protection (circuit breakers + load shedding) to keep the platform within SLOs and degrade safely when the system is saturated.

**Primary outcomes**
- Reject or degrade traffic early and predictably to prevent overload cascades.
- Provide customer-facing errors with stable semantics (status codes, error codes, retry guidance, correlation IDs).
- Enable safe degradation modes (fallback routing, reduced features, queued admission) while preserving tenant isolation.
- Reduce incident count/severity for capacity-related and dependency-related failure modes.
- Improve...

title: Hosted API SLO: Overload Exceptions and Error Budget Policy
preview:
## Purpose
This page defines how *overload-induced* failures are categorized for Hosted API SLO measurement, how they impact error budgets, and how we communicate the impact (internally and externally). It aligns SRE, Product, Support, and Engineering on what “counts” vs what is classified separately.

This policy a...
text:
## Purpose
This page defines how *overload-induced* failures are categorized for Hosted API SLO measurement, how they impact error budgets, and how we communicate the impact (internally and externally). It aligns SRE, Product, Support, and Engineering on what “counts” vs what is classified separately.

This policy applies to the **Hosted API** (multi-tenant) request path, including gateway admission control, Smart Routing fallback, and serving runtime backpressure.

---

## Summary (what counts)
**Overload errors are user-visible failures and count against the Hosted API availability SLO for paid Hosted tiers.**

We additionally:
- Classify overload separately from generic 5xx to improve reporting and to ensure controlled degradation is preferred over timeouts.
- Track an internal “overload share” guardrail to prevent normalizing load shedding as steady-state behavior.
- Treat *custom...

title: Status Page Transparency Upgrades — Project Brief
preview:
## Summary
Redwood’s current public status reporting is too coarse for how customers experience incidents (e.g., “API is fine” while Embeddings or Streaming is degraded in a single region). This project upgrades our public status page and incident communications to be more precise, consistent, and actionable without...
text:
## Summary
Redwood’s current public status reporting is too coarse for how customers experience incidents (e.g., “API is fine” while Embeddings or Streaming is degraded in a single region). This project upgrades our public status page and incident communications to be more precise, consistent, and actionable without increasing on-call burden or disclosing customer-specific capacity constraints.

This initiative delivers:
- A clear, owned component taxonomy (product surface × region × capability where appropriate).
- Standard incident communication templates aligned to incident phases.
- A publishable post-incident summary format that can be produced quickly and safely.
- Automation and signal mapping so component statuses are accurate, reviewable, and less manual.

## Goals (in scope)
### 1) Improve status page component granularity
- Define customer-facing components aligned to produ...

title: Runbook: KMS decrypt / envelope decryption failures
preview:
## Purpose
This runbook helps on-call engineers diagnose and mitigate **KMS decrypt / envelope decryption failures** affecting Redwood Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments. It covers:
- Symptoms and impact assessment
- Dashboards/metrics to consult
- Log and trace queries
- Common root causes (permissions, thr...
text:
## Purpose
This runbook helps on-call engineers diagnose and mitigate **KMS decrypt / envelope decryption failures** affecting Redwood Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments. It covers:
- Symptoms and impact assessment
- Dashboards/metrics to consult
- Log and trace queries
- Common root causes (permissions, throttling, timeouts, wrong key ID, rotation/cutover issues)
- Mitigation steps and safe rollbacks
- Escalation and communications

This runbook assumes we use the standardized KMS wrapper and error taxonomy (see ADR-007).

---

## Scope & definitions
**Envelope encryption (simplified):**
- A data encryption key (DEK) encrypts payloads at rest/in storage.
- The DEK is encrypted (wrapped) by a key encryption key (KEK) in a cloud KMS/HSM.
- At read time, services call KMS to decrypt/unwrap the DEK, then decrypt the payload.

**What counts as a KMS decrypt failure incident:**
- A...

title: Status Page Transparency: SLO & Error Budget Alignment (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private)

It also provides guidance for handling common mismatches (public impact without SLO burn, or SLO ...
text:
## Purpose
This document defines how **public status page component states** should relate to Redwood Inference’s **internal SLOs and error budgets** across deployment modes (**Hosted API**, **Dedicated**, **Private**). It also provides guidance for handling common mismatches (public impact without SLO burn, or SLO burn without clear customer impact).

This is part of the Status Page Transparency Upgrades initiative and is intended for:
- SRE on-call and Incident Commanders (IC)
- Comms Leads / customer-facing incident writers
- Observability and service owners defining SLOs and alerts

## Non-goals
- Defining contractual SLAs or guaranteeing customer outcomes. (Public status is **not** an SLA statement.)
- Exhaustively listing every internal SLO. This doc focuses on the alignment patterns and the minimum required signal coverage per public component.

## Key principle: Status Page ≠ ...

title: Provision-to-Ready SLOs, SLIs, Error Budget, and Alerting Policy
preview:

This page defines the production SLOs and error budget policy for the Provision-to-Ready automation for GPU fleet nodes used by Redwood Dedicated and shared capacity pools.

The intent is to:
- Make "time-to-ready" measurable and comparable across regions/GPU types.

text:
## Purpose
This page defines the production SLOs and error budget policy for the Provision-to-Ready automation for GPU fleet nodes used by Redwood Dedicated and shared capacity pools.

The intent is to:
- Make "time-to-ready" measurable and comparable across regions/GPU types.
- Set explicit reliability targets for automated provisioning and post-provision validation.
- Establish clear alert thresholds and escalation criteria so on-call responders can act quickly.

This doc is normative for alerting and error budget enforcement. Implementation details for the state machine and event sources are defined in ADR-017.

---
## Definitions
### Readiness states (canonical)
Nodes transition through:
- Allocated
- Provisioning
- Validating
- Ready
- Quarantined

A node is considered available for inference workloads only when it reaches **Ready**.

### What "time-to-ready" means
**Time-to-read...

## Purpose
This page provides recommended **Service Level Objectives (SLOs)** for **Redwood Private** installations (VPC and on-prem). It also explains how the **Private Observability Minimum Pack** alert rules map to those SLOs (including severity and paging guidance).

title: Dedicated Autoscaling: SLO & Alerting Updates (Bounds + Predictive Mode)
preview:
## Summary
This page defines SLO and alerting updates for **Redwood Dedicated autoscaling** after introducing:
- **Customer-configurable min/max bounds** (hard constraints)

The goal is to ensure on-call has *...
text:
## Summary
This page defines SLO and alerting updates for **Redwood Dedicated autoscaling** after introducing:
- **Customer-configurable min/max bounds** (hard constraints)
- **Predictive scaling mode** (forecast-informed desired capacity), with reactive override where applicable

The goal is to ensure on-call has *clear, actionable* indicators when:
- A deployment is saturating due to hitting **max bound**
- A deployment is **unable to meet min bound** due to regional capacity shortage
- Predictive inputs are **stale/incorrect** and impacting performance
- The autoscaler is **oscillating** or stuck

This doc includes:
- New SLIs/SLOs and how we measure them
- Paging vs ticket alert rules and thresholds
- Error budget / rollout policy for pilot → GA

---

## Scope
**In scope**
- Dedicated deployments with autoscaler policy enabled (bounds + optional predictive mode)
- Autoscaler loop ...

title: Runbook: Burst Pool Overload Mitigation (Dedicated Burst Capacity)
preview:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to detect, diagnose, mitigate, and communicate during **Burst Pool Overload** events for **Dedicated Burst Capacity**.

A burst pool overload is any situation where shared Redwood-managed burst pools (per region + hardware class) become saturated or unstable, and/or where **non-...
text:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to detect, diagnose, mitigate, and communicate during **Burst Pool Overload** events for **Dedicated Burst Capacity**.

A burst pool overload is any situation where shared Redwood-managed burst pools (per region + hardware class) become saturated or unstable, and/or where **non-burst tenant SLOs are threatened** by burst activity. The runbook prioritizes:
1) Protecting baseline Dedicated SLOs for all tenants (including non-burst tenants)
2) Restoring stable operation of the burst pool
3) Minimizing customer-impact and providing clear customer-facing explanations

## Scope
**In scope:** overload/saturation of shared burst pools, admission controller instability, telemetry lag causing unfair denials, routing amplification, global fleet safety cap triggers.

**Out of scope:** GPU host-level failures unrelated to burst pools (see Fleet hardware runboo...

# Purpose
This page summarizes all production incidents in H1 2025 where the dominant failure mode was **GPU capacity exhaustion** or **quota exhaustion** (including cases where quota prevented provisioning). The goal is to validate completeness of paging signals and ensure incidents are owned consistently.

# Inclusion criteria
- Production customer impact
- Primary failure mode: capacity exhaustion, quota exhaustion, or provisioning blocked by quota
- Occurred between 2025-01-01 and 2025-06-30

title: Dedicated Burst Capacity: SLOs, Error Budgets, and Alerting Policy
preview:
## Purpose
This page defines Service Level Objectives (SLOs), error budgets, and alerting policy for **Dedicated Burst Capacity**.

The guiding principle is: **burst must never regress baseline Dedicated reliability for non-bursting tenants**. Bursting is a paid add-on and may be denied; denial is not, by itself, a ...
text:
## Purpose
This page defines Service Level Objectives (SLOs), error budgets, and alerting policy for **Dedicated Burst Capacity**.

The guiding principle is: **burst must never regress baseline Dedicated reliability for non-bursting tenants**. Bursting is a paid add-on and may be denied; denial is not, by itself, a breach as long as denial behavior is consistent with documented limits and the platform remains reliable.

This document is normative for:
- What we protect (non-burst tenants first)
- What we measure (SLIs and metric sources)
- When we page (and when we ticket)
- Rollout gates for beta/GA by region and hardware class

## Scope
### In scope
- Dedicated requests served from reserved capacity (baseline)
- Dedicated requests served using burst pools (burst path)
- Admission controller behavior and routing integration
- Fleet safety caps and global circuit breaker behavior

###...

text:
## Purpose
This runbook covers alerts and common failure modes that occur during **Redwood Private** upgrades (VPC and on-prem/air-gapped). It provides:
- Fast triage steps to distinguish expected upgrade noise vs. actionable incidents.
- Mitigation playbooks for the most common alert categories (error rate spikes, DB migration issues, control-plane connectivity, capacity/resource pressure, and certificate/identity problems).
- Clear escalation and rollback decision points.

This runbook is intended for **SRE on-call** and release captain support during planned upgrade windows.

---

## Scope
**In scope**
- Alerts triggered during an upgrade window for a single Private environment.
- Installer-driven upgrades (redwood-private-installer), Helm upgrades, control-plane/data-plane rollouts.
- Post-upgrade verification and “upgrade stuck” scenarios.

**Out of scope**
- Initial install fail...

title: Runbook: Audit Exporter Delivery Failures (SIEM-ready audit export beta)

text:
## Purpose
This runbook covers on-call response for **audit-log-exporter** delivery issues impacting the SIEM-ready audit export beta (Splunk HEC, Datadog Logs intake, and S3 delivery). It includes common failure modes, triage steps, mitigations (pause/resume exports, credential rotation, DLQ handling), and customer communications guidance.

This integration is **at-least-once** delivery. Downstream consumers must deduplicate using `event_id` (see ADR).

## Scope
**In scope:** Hosted and Dedicated environments using the managed exporter service.

**Out of scope for beta:** Private/on-prem deployments (documented follow-on).

## Definitions
- **Exporter**: `audit-log-exporter` service responsible for reading audit events and delivering to customer destinations.
- **Destination**: One of Splunk HEC, Datadog Logs, or S3.
- **Lag**: Time between audit event creation (`event_ts`) and succe...

## Purpose
This page defines the canonical Service Level Objective (SLO) for **Dedicated throughput** at Redwood Inference, including:
- SLI and metric semantics (tokens/sec as canonical; when requests/sec is used)
- Measurement windows and aggregation
- Exclusions and “not counted” conditions
- Burn-rate alerting and error budget policy

This is the formal reference used by SRE, Observability, Infra/Fleet, and Product when implementing dashboards, alerts, incident classification, and customer-facing reporting.

## Scope
### Applies to
- **Dedicated deployments** (hosted in Redwood-managed infrastructure) with a **committed throughput floor** recorded in control plane configuration.
- Deployments where a customer has:
 - A pinned **model family + major version** (e.g., `llama-3.x`, `mistral-large.x`) and
 - A configured **max context length** wit...

title: Dedicated Tier Capacity Model (v1): Headroom, Overbooking, and Burst Pools
preview:
## Overview
This document defines Redwood’s capacity planning methodology for **Dedicated** tier commitments (Standard / Plus / Premium). It explains:
- How we translate **throughput SLO floors** into **provisioned GPU capacity**.
- Required **headroom targets** by tier (and why they differ).
- The **overbooking pol...
text:
## Overview
This document defines Redwood’s capacity planning methodology for **Dedicated** tier commitments (Standard / Plus / Premium). It explains:
- How we translate **throughput SLO floors** into **provisioned GPU capacity**.
- Required **headroom targets** by tier (and why they differ).
- The **overbooking policy** (what is allowed vs prohibited).
- How we size and operate **burst pools**.
- Planning assumptions for **demand variability** (model mix, context length, traffic burstiness).

The intent is to make Dedicated commitments predictable for customers while keeping fleet utilization and unit economics manageable.

**Primary owners**
- Capacity model + forecasting: Jamal Pierce (SRE Capacity Planning)
- Fleet supply + shape availability: Vanessa Ortiz, Irene Choi (Infra/Fleet)
- SLO semantics and enforcement: Sean Gallagher, Rafael Mendes (SRE)
- Margin guardrails: Laura Ben...

text:
## Purpose
This page defines the reliability targets and escalation mechanics for the **Runtime LTS channel** across **Hosted API**, **Dedicated**, and **Private** deployments.

It answers:
- What SLOs we commit to for LTS traffic (and what we measure).
- What happens when we are burning error budget (who gets paged, who decides, and what the required actions are).
- When an LTS patch is **Mandatory** vs **Recommended/Optional**, and how we communicate that to Support/CS/Sales.

## Scope
**In scope**
- Runtime LTS branches (e.g., `runtime-1.18-lts`) and all published patch releases (e.g., `1.18.1-lts`).
- LTS traffic in Hosted and Dedicated.
- LTS artifacts installed via Redwood Private (VPC/on-prem) when customer is pinned to an LTS patch.

**Out of scope**
- Mainline runtime SLOs and escalation (covered elsewhere).
- Feature delivery expectations on LTS (LTS prioritizes stability; n...

text:
## Purpose
This page maps common SLO burn patterns to the *minimum actionable diagnostics* that Incident Bot v2 should surface in the “first 5 minutes” and the dashboards responders should open next.

Primary use cases:
- Reduce time-to-triage by making SLO symptoms immediately lead to likely causes (deploy regression vs misconfig vs saturation/capacity).
- Standardize what we look at across Hosted API, Dedicated pools, and Private deployments.
- Keep bot-driven queries bounded and resilient (prefer links + cached context when live queries are slow).

Non-goals:
- Replace runbooks. This page points to runbooks and dashboards; it does not fully enumerate mitigations.

---

## How to use this mapping during an incident
1) Identify the dominant symptom (what is burning?): **latency**, **5xx**, **streaming disconnects**, **timeouts**, or **region-only degradation**.
2) In the incident thr...

text:
## Purpose
This page defines the SLO signals, cohort-specific metrics, and alerting guidance for **Hosted canary rollouts** (percentage-based traffic splitting on Hosted routes).

The goal is to:
- Detect real customer impact quickly (error/latency/timeouts) **per cohort** (baseline vs canary).
- Provide safe defaults for **auto-stop/auto-rollback** wiring without creating alert storms.
- Standardize dashboard panels and queries used during beta and GA.

This page is SRE-owned, but it is intended for product engineers, on-call responders, and support.

## Scope
Applies to:
- **Hosted routes** using the canary rollout workflow.
- Cohort tagging emitted by the data plane and aggregated by telemetry.

Does not cover:
- Dedicated/Private rollouts (separate SLO policy; signals may be similar but dimensions differ).
- Deep quality-eval gates (optional/manual in GA; future automated gates wi...

title: Route Experiments Control Plane SLOs & Error Budgets
preview:

This page defines Service Level Objectives (SLOs), Service Level Indicators (SLIs), and error budget policies for the Route Experiments control plane and its config propagation path into the routing/data plane.

Route Experiments is part of the Console “Rollouts” surface area and enables route-level A/B/n...
text:
## Purpose
This page defines Service Level Objectives (SLOs), Service Level Indicators (SLIs), and error budget policies for the Route Experiments control plane and its config propagation path into the routing/data plane.

Route Experiments is part of the Console “Rollouts” surface area and enables route-level A/B/n testing with deterministic assignment and guardrails. Reliability here is primarily about:
- **API availability** for managing experiments (Console + automation)
- **Config propagation freshness** so routing decisions match the intended experiment configuration
- **Safe degradation** when the control plane or propagation path is impaired

This document is used by SRE + owning engineering teams to:
- Set expectations for beta/GA
- Drive alerting and on-call runbooks
- Provide change-management gating using error budgets

## In-scope components
### Control plane (primary)
- ...

title: Cache Efficiency Guardrail SLO Proposal (Prefix + KV Cache)
preview:
## Summary
This page proposes **internal cache-efficiency guardrail SLOs** for Redwood’s prefix cache and KV cache telemetry. These are **not customer-facing SLAs**. The intent is to:

- Detect regressions that materially increase cost/latency (e.g., prefix cache disabled, version mismatch causing systematic misses,...
text:
## Summary
This page proposes **internal cache-efficiency guardrail SLOs** for Redwood’s prefix cache and KV cache telemetry. These are **not customer-facing SLAs**. The intent is to:

- Detect regressions that materially increase cost/latency (e.g., prefix cache disabled, version mismatch causing systematic misses, eviction pressure spikes).
- Provide consistent triage signals for SRE + Runtime + Telemetry.
- Avoid paging on **expected workload shifts** (prompt mix changes, model/route traffic migrations, new customer onboarding).

Guardrail SLOs are evaluated per environment (prod, canary), deployment mode (Hosted/Dedicated/Private), and region with strict cardinality controls.

## Background / Why now
We are rolling out new cache telemetry (hit/miss counters, miss-reason taxonomy, eviction/pressure indicators, and wasted prefill). This telemetry enables us to:

- Separate “cache go...

title: Reliability Review: Repeated Tensor-Parallel (TP) Timeouts and Stall Events
preview:
## Summary
Over the past 4–6 weeks we observed a repeating pattern of request timeouts and “hung” replicas in multi-GPU tensor-parallel (TP) deployments, concentrated in high-throughput Dedicated clusters and a small number of Hosted clusters running TP replicas under bursty load.

While individual incidents present...
text:
## Summary
Over the past 4–6 weeks we observed a repeating pattern of request timeouts and “hung” replicas in multi-GPU tensor-parallel (TP) deployments, concentrated in high-throughput Dedicated clusters and a small number of Hosted clusters running TP replicas under bursty load.

While individual incidents presented as different customer-visible symptoms (streaming timeouts, elevated p99 latency, occasional 5xx/504 spikes), investigation strongly suggests a shared class of TP stall modes:
- Rank-level stalls inside or around NCCL collectives (silent hangs rather than explicit NCCL errors)
- Scheduler ordering / fairness issues that can starve collectives under mixed prefill/decode
- Edge cases where cancellation and/or KV cache compaction under memory pressure creates rank divergence

This doc consolidates observed signals, contributing factors, and SRE-driven reliability actions (a...

title: Dedicated rollout gates: SLO alignment and error-budget-based promotion policy

text:
## Purpose
This page defines the **default rollout gate thresholds** for **Dedicated staged rollouts** and maps them explicitly to Dedicated **availability, latency, and capacity (saturation/headroom)** SLOs.

It also documents the **error-budget-based promotion policy** used by SRE and the rollout policy engine to decide whether a rollout stage can be promoted automatically.

This doc is intended for:
- SRE on-call and SRE stakeholders supporting Dedicated
- Platform + Routing engineers implementing gate evaluation and promotion semantics
- Product/Console teams who surface gate evidence and promotion constraints

## Scope
In-scope gates (v1):
- Request success/error rates (5xx, 429, dropped requests)
- Latency (p95/p99)
- Saturation/headroom (GPU utilization, queue depth, token throughput headroom)
- GPU health (node taints/ECC/throttling summarized at pool partition level)
- OOM / ...

title: Incident Bot Diagnostics v2 — Design
preview:
## Summary
Incident Bot Diagnostics v2 upgrades Redwood’s incident Slack bot to automatically attach standardized “first 5 minutes” context in #incidents and #eng-oncall. v2 adds three primary context areas:
1) **Recent deploy context** (service/version/rollout phase/canary + linked PRs)
2) **Customer/tenant-safe co...
text:
## Summary
Incident Bot Diagnostics v2 upgrades Redwood’s incident Slack bot to automatically attach standardized “first 5 minutes” context in #incidents and #eng-oncall. v2 adds three primary context areas:
1) **Recent deploy context** (service/version/rollout phase/canary + linked PRs)
2) **Customer/tenant-safe config diffs** (feature flags, routing policy, quotas, model pins) with redaction + audited access
3) **Saturation signals** (GPU utilization, KV cache pressure, queue depth, scheduler/backpressure indicators, regional capacity) plus deep links to dashboards/traces

This document covers goals, architecture, data sources, Slack UX, caching and rate limiting, failure modes, and rollout plan.

---

## Goals
- Reduce **time-to-triage** and **time-to-mitigation** for common incident classes:
 - Deploy regressions
 - Misconfiguration (flags/routing/quotas/model pins)
 - Regional ca...

title: Runbook: Backup/Restore Smoke Test (On-Call) — Redwood Private
preview:

This runbook is used for:
- Weekly (or per-customer requirement) integrity checks of the most recent control-plane backup.

text:
## Purpose
This runbook describes the periodic smoke test SRE performs to ensure Redwood Private backup artifacts are usable and that a restore into a staging environment is repeatable.

This runbook is used for:
- Weekly (or per-customer requirement) integrity checks of the most recent control-plane backup.
- Scheduled restore drills into a dedicated staging cluster.
- Alerting and paging guidance when checks/drills fail.

## Scope
In scope (smoke test):
- Validate the **latest control-plane backup** exists, can be decrypted, and passes integrity checks.
- Perform a **restore drill into staging** using the supported installer workflow.
- Run post-restore validation (health, API checks, basic tenancy/config verification).
- Record drill duration and outcomes; trigger paging/escalation per policy.

Out of scope:
- Full region-loss DR execution (see DR plans).
- Cluster-level recovery (...

## Purpose
This page defines the **SLOs, SLIs, alerting, and escalation paths** for the Unit Economics pipeline powering the Internal Unit Economics Dashboard.

The metric is executive-visible (Finance + Product + Engineering) and is used for quarter-to-date reporting. The pipeline must be reliable enough for weekly exec readouts while still allowing iteration on attribution logic.

## Scope
**In scope:**
- Token metering inputs (with emphasis on **output tokens**, including streaming paths)
- Hourly aggregation job + warehouse tables/views used by the dashboard
- Dimension coverage: model/version, region, customer tier, quantization_profile, streaming flag
- Daily reconciliation view vs billing and Finance rate card inputs

**Out of scope:**
- Console/UI availability SLOs
- External customer-facing reporting

title: Traffic mirroring for SLO validation and capacity-impact testing
preview:
Summary:
This playbook describes a standardized approach to using traffic mirroring (traffic shadowing) to validate SLO compliance and measure capacity impact without affecting production traffic. It focuses on safe mirroring pipelines, measurement methodology, telemetry collection, and decision gates for progressin...
text:
Summary:
This playbook describes a standardized approach to using traffic mirroring (traffic shadowing) to validate SLO compliance and measure capacity impact without affecting production traffic. It focuses on safe mirroring pipelines, measurement methodology, telemetry collection, and decision gates for progressing from shadow tests to gradual rollouts.

Why this exists:
- SLO-driven decisions require realistic load and production-like request patterns.
- Full canaries change user-visible state; mirroring lets teams exercise the stack end-to-end in a read-only fashion.
- Mirroring enables: regression detection, resource usage profiling (CPU/GPU/memory), KV/DB read amplification analysis, and cache-hit behavior insights.

Scope and applicability:
- Services backed by stateless inference endpoints (text-gen, embeddings) and stateful control planes are both supported, but write-side ef...

preview:

## When this review is required
SRE review is required for any change that is classified Medium/High/Critical risk ...
text:
## Purpose
This document defines the minimum SRE review steps for planned changes that may impact availability, latency, or throughput SLOs for any production environment (Hosted, Dedicated, Private).

## When this review is required
SRE review is required for any change that is classified Medium/High/Critical risk under the Production Change Management Policy, or any change that:
- Requires disabling alerts
- Requires a maintenance window
- Has a credible risk of increased 5xx, latency regression, or capacity shortfall

## Required checks (attach evidence/links)
### 1) Current SLO state
- Confirm current 30-day error budget remaining for impacted services/tenants.
- If error budget remaining is below 25%, attach an explicit risk acceptance from Engineering + SRE lead.

### 2) Capacity + saturation
- Confirm GPU and request capacity headroom for the expected window.
- Confirm no overl...

title: Incident Hypothesis Library and Repro Environment Specification
preview:
Summary:
This page defines a canonical hypothesis taxonomy for inference and control-plane incidents, and a minimal reproducible environment spec to validate hypotheses during incident review and troubleshooting. The goal is to reduce time-to-evidence, make postmortems actionable, and ensure SLO/error-budget implica...
text:
Summary:
This page defines a canonical hypothesis taxonomy for inference and control-plane incidents, and a minimal reproducible environment spec to validate hypotheses during incident review and troubleshooting. The goal is to reduce time-to-evidence, make postmortems actionable, and ensure SLO/error-budget implications are clearly mapped to every hypothesis and remediation path.

Why this exists:
- Multiple teams repeatedly re-create ad-hoc repros that are not portable or auditable.
- Lack of a shared hypothesis taxonomy makes aggregation of past incidents difficult.
- Decision-making during incidents is slower when engineers must re-derive the minimal telemetry and environment needed to validate a root cause.

Scope:
- Applies to inference-serving incidents and associated control-plane failures (scheduling/allocation/rollback).
- Not a replacement for full-scale load or chaos exper...

title: Failure-domain-aware capacity scheduling and SLO-safe deployments
preview:
Overview:
This playbook describes a failure-domain-aware approach to capacity scheduling and deployment gating designed to protect SLOs and preserve error budget during planned and unplanned failures. It combines quantitative headroom provisioning, progressive rollouts, and automated rollback/eviction rules that are...
text:
Overview:
This playbook describes a failure-domain-aware approach to capacity scheduling and deployment gating designed to protect SLOs and preserve error budget during planned and unplanned failures. It combines quantitative headroom provisioning, progressive rollouts, and automated rollback/eviction rules that are failure-domain–sensitive (region / AZ / rack / GPU-pool / node). The goal is to keep user-visible SLOs stable while making predictable capacity changes for model serving and multi-tenant workloads.

Scope:
- Applies to all production inference pools managed by Redwood (hosted, dedicated, private control plane).
- Primary targets: text generation, chat, and embedding routes where p95 latency and availability SLOs are observable.
- Excludes: offline batch/ETL jobs (handled by batch capacity playbook).

Key goals:
- Ensure SLO preservation during single and multi-domain degra...

title: Change Freeze and Risk Assessment (SLO/Error Budget Aware)
preview:
h1. Summary
This page defines when Redwood enforces change freezes or heightened risk review based on live reliability posture (SLO compliance and error budget burn). These rules apply to production changes for Hosted API and Console.

h1. Why this exists
When services are degraded, deployments often increase custom...
text:
h1. Summary
This page defines when Redwood enforces change freezes or heightened risk review based on live reliability posture (SLO compliance and error budget burn). These rules apply to production changes for Hosted API and Console.

h1. Why this exists
When services are degraded, deployments often increase customer impact and make diagnosis harder. Error-budget-aware change control prioritizes stability while preserving a clear path for urgent fixes.

h1. Inputs used for decision
* SLO compliance for the last 7 and 28 days
* Error budget remaining for the active SLO period
* Current incident status (active Sev 0/1/2)

h1. Change control states
|| State || Trigger || Allowed changes || Required approvals ||
| Green | No active Sev0/1 and \u2265 60% error budget remaining | Normal changes allowed | Peer + service owner (plus security/SRE if applicable) |
| Yellow | \u003c 60% and \u2...

preview:

# Scope

text:
# Purpose
This runbook describes how to manually shift (fail over) production inference traffic for the Hosted API between regions or clusters during GPU capacity shortages, elevated error rates, or regional degradation.

# Scope
- Applies to: chat + embeddings endpoints served by Redwood API (Hosted).
- Not for: Redwood Dedicated customer-specific pools (see dedicated incident playbooks).

# Triggers (choose one)
- Sustained 5xx > 1% for 10+ minutes in a region
- p95 latency > 2x baseline for 15+ minutes with correlated GPU saturation
- GPU capacity deficit (queue depth increasing; admission control engaged)
- Cloud provider incident impacting a region

# Preconditions
- Incident declared or oncall lead approval
- Status page draft ready (if customer-visible)
- Confirm target region has headroom (GPU free capacity + router capacity)

# Definitions
- "Traffic shift": changing routing ...

title: Service stability contracts and capacity negotiation manual

Overview
This document describes the standard contract template, negotiation procedure, and evidence requirements used by SRE and product teams when agreeing service stability commitments (SLOs + Error Budgets) and capacity reservations for production inference services. It centralizes expectations for measurable SL...
text:
Overview
This document describes the standard contract template, negotiation procedure, and evidence requirements used by SRE and product teams when agreeing service stability commitments (SLOs + Error Budgets) and capacity reservations for production inference services. It centralizes expectations for measurable SLAs, capacity forecasting, billing-reconciled error-budget allocation, and a repeatable escalation path for emergency capacity events.

Goals
- Make SLO/Capacity commitments explicit, measurable, and time-boxed.
- Ensure capacity negotiations are evidence-led and prioritize customer/business impact.
- Provide an operational template for transient and long-term capacity changes.
- Reduce ambiguity during incidents with pre-agreed fallback and escalation lanes.

Audience
- Service owners and product managers negotiating SLOs.
- SREs responsible for capacity planning and oncall...
